main_ingredients: biryani spices (turmeric, cumin, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of fried calamari biryani is approximately 550 g, comprising a generous mound of spiced basmati rice topped with several pieces of breaded deep-fried calamari.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Biryani rice dish with seafood (composite estimate) and fried squid / calamari component
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for fried calamari biryani as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of: (1) USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked basmati rice (approximately 55% by weight), (2) USDA FoodData Central entry for breaded fried squid/calamari (approximately 35% by weight), and (3) biryani masala and ghee components (approximately 10% by weight). Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite estimate and found broadly consistent; values retained with medium confidence. Singapore HPB database consulted but no direct biryani-with-calamari entry found. ICMR-NIN cited in input record supports general biryani spice and rice composition context.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Basmati rice, white, boiled
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for fried calamari biryani as a composite dish. GI estimated at 55 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white basmati rice, which has a published GI of approximately 50–58 (mean ~55) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The breaded calamari component contributes additional starch from breadcrumbs but represents a minority of total carbohydrate. The protein (8.2 g/100g) and fat (8.2 g/100g) content of the composite dish moderates postprandial glucose response relative to plain rice. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (17.27 g/100g × 550 g / 100) / 100 = 55 × 94.99 / 100 ≈ 52.2 g, rounded to 52.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 327 mg/100g; a 550 g plate delivers approximately 1,800 mg sodium, approaching 78% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single meal.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 200–250 g and request reduced-salt preparation, as a full plate provides close to the daily sodium ceiling; avoid adding soy sauce, fish sauce, or other salty condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate GI (~55) from basmati rice and breadcrumb coating; a 550 g plate delivers approximately 95 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~52.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit the serving to 150–200 g to keep the glycemic load manageable; the protein (8.2 g/100g) and fat (8.2 g/100g) content help moderate postprandial glucose rise, but the large standard portion size poses a significant carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds oxidised fats and increases total fat to 8.2 g/100g; cholesterol is 63.6 mg/100g; a full plate delivers approximately 350 mg cholesterol and 45 g total fat.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose a smaller portion (150–200 g) or request grilled calamari biryani to reduce oxidised fat and cholesterol intake; the saturated fat content (1.82 g/100g) is moderate but the deep-frying method generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that are unfavourable for cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates AGEs that directly promote joint inflammation; this triggers the RA deep-fry hard gate.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard deep-fried form and request grilled or steamed calamari biryani instead; the biryani spice profile (turmeric, ginger, cumin, cinnamon) is strongly anti-inflammatory and beneficial if the preparation method is changed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fried Calamari Biryani (also known as Sotong Biryani in Singapore) is a fusion rice dish combining the aromatic layered technique of South Asian biryani with deep-fried breaded calamari (squid) as the primary protein. The basmati rice is slow-cooked with whole spices including cardamom, cinnamon, cloves, cumin, and turmeric, and layered with caramelised onions, tomatoes, yogurt, ginger, garlic, and fresh mint and coriander. The calamari pieces are coated in seasoned breadcrumbs and deep-fried until golden and crisp before being served atop or alongside the fragrant rice. The dish delivers a contrast of textures — soft, fluffy spiced rice against the crunchy exterior and tender interior of the fried squid. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides approximately 173 kcal, 8.2 g protein, 8.2 g fat, and 17.3 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 327 mg/100g, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low) based on the basmati rice component, but the large standard serving size (550 g) results in a high glycemic load of approximately 52. The deep-frying method generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), making this dish unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis. It is halal-certified in most Singapore hawker and restaurant contexts. The dish is popular in Singapore's Indian-Muslim food culture and is commonly found on GrabFood and other delivery platforms.
common_user_queries: How many calories in fried calamari biryani?
Is fried squid biryani good for high blood pressure?
Is sotong biryani suitable for rheumatoid arthritis?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for fried calamari biryani as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from a weighted blend of USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked basmati rice and breaded fried squid, cross-checked against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Values are broadly consistent with expected composition for this dish type. GI is estimated from the basmati rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.

The input record cited ICMR-NIN as a source supporting menu_listing context; ICMR-NIN data was used for general biryani spice and rice composition context only. Primary nutrient values are estimated composites from USDA FoodData Central. The ethnic_tags in the input record included 'Naan' which is not a component of this dish and has been excluded.
